Such protocols simplify comparison but hide the conditions that dominate real monitoring networks: uneven global coverage, structured missingness, heterogeneous pollutant scales, and deployment cost. We introduce \textbf{AirQualityBench}, a global multi-pollutant benchmark designed to evaluate forecasting models under these realistic conditions. The benchmark contains hourly observations from 3,720 monitoring stations over 2021--2025, covers six major pollutants, and preserves provider-native observation masks. Rather than imputing a dense data tensor, AirQualityBench exposes missingness as part of the forecasting problem and reports errors on valid future observations after inverse transformation to physical concentration scales. Evaluating representative spatio-temporal models under this unified protocol shows that strong performance on sanitized datasets does not reliably transfer to global, fragmented monitoring streams. AirQualityBench therefore serves as a realistic testbed for scalable, mask-aware, and physically interpretable air-quality forecasting. All benchmark data, code, evaluation scripts, and baseline implementations are available at \href{https://github.com/Star-Learning/AirQualityBench}{GitHub}.

AI.tldr: AirQualityBench introduces a global multi-pollutant benchmark for realistic air-quality forecasting, addressing challenges like uneven data coverage and structured missingness.
AI.motivation: Existing air-quality forecasting protocols often use sanitized, normalized datasets, which fail to reflect real-world conditions with uneven coverage and missing observations.
AI.method: AirQualityBench utilizes data from 3,720 monitoring stations (2021–2025), preserving native observation masks and reporting errors after reverting predictions to physical pollutant scales.
AI.result: Strong forecasting models built on sanitized datasets fail to perform under realistic conditions, demonstrating the significance of AirQualityBench for true scalability and physical interpretability.
AI.conclusion: AirQualityBench provides a unified, realistic framework to evaluate and develop air-quality forecasting models under real-world conditions.

title: On the Blessing of Pre-training in Weak-to-Strong Generalization
summary: The paradigm of Weak-to-Strong Generalization (W2SG) suggests that a pre-trained strong model can surpass its weak supervisor, yet the decisive role of pre-training remains theoretically and empirically under-explored. In this work, we identify pre-training as the essential prerequisite for the emergence of W2SG. Theoretically, we formalize the W2SG problem within a high-dimensional single-index model framework using spiked Gaussian data, modeling pre-training as a spectral initialization step. Building upon prior impossibility results regarding the failure of learning under random initialization, we prove that W2SG is achievable when pre-training provides a geometric warm start that places the model within an "effective region" characterized by a perturbed strong-convexity geometry. Within this region, we derive a rigorous generalization bound that naturally captures the optimization dynamics: an initial performance improvement followed by a saturation bottleneck dictated by the weak supervisor's bias. Empirically, we first validate all our assumptions and theoretical insights through controlled synthetic simulations. Finally, through a massive-scale evaluation of hundreds of intermediate pre-training checkpoints from large language models, we demonstrate that W2SG is not an innate capability but emerges via a phase transition tightly coupled with the progression of pre-training.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the critical role of pre-training in enabling Weak-to-Strong Generalization (W2SG), developing a theoretical framework and validating its assumptions via simulations and large-scale evaluations.
AI.motivation: To understand the decisive role of pre-training in advancing Weak-to-Strong Generalization (W2SG), which has been insufficiently explored both theoretically and empirically.
AI.method: The method involves formalizing W2SG within a high-dimensional single-index model using spiked Gaussian data, modeling pre-training as spectral initialization, and validating observations through synthetic simulations and large-scale evaluations of pre-training checkpoints.
AI.result: The paper provides theoretical proof that pre-training ensures W2SG when it positions models into an effective region with perturbed strong-convexity geometry. Empirical results confirm the emergence of W2SG via a phase transition during pre-training progression.
AI.conclusion: Pre-training acts as a critical prerequisite for W2SG, with its emergence hinging upon the progression of pre-training. Both theoretical and experimental evidence support this assertion.

title: Algospeak, Hiding in the Open: The Trade-off Between Legible Meaning and Detection Avoidance
summary: As large language models (LLMs) increasingly mediate both content generation and moderation, linguistic evasion strategies known as Algospeak have intensified the coevolution between evaders and detectors. This research formalizes the underlying dynamics grounded in a joint action model: when Algospeak increases, detectability and understandability decrease. Further, the concept of Majority Understandable Modulation (MUM) is introduced and defined as the modulation level at which additional evasive alteration increases detector evasion but loses comprehension for the majority of recipients. To empirically probe this trade-off, we introduce a reproducible framework that can be used to create meaning-preserving, Algospeak-style variants, based on an existing taxonomy and with tunable modulation levels. Using COVID-19 disinformation as a first proof-by-example setting, we construct a reference dataset of 700 modulated items, drawn from twenty base sentences across five modulation levels and seven strategies. We then run two linked evaluations with seven different language models: one testing for interpretation through meaning recovery and one for disinformation detection through classification. Curve fitting over modulation levels yields an estimate of the Majority Understandable Modulation threshold and enables sensitivity analyses across strategies and models, see Figure 1. Results reveal the characteristic relationships between understandability and modulation. This study lays the groundwork for understanding the dynamics behind Algospeak and provides the framework, dataset, and experimental setups described.

AI.tldr: The study explores how Algospeak, evasive linguistic strategies, affects content moderation and comprehension. It introduces a trade-off concept, "Majority Understandable Modulation," and offers a dataset and framework for testing modulated language's impact on meaning and moderation.
AI.motivation: As large language models mediate content generation and moderation, understanding the dynamics between evasive language (Algospeak) and detection is crucial to improve both content moderation and communication clarity.
AI.method: The researchers developed a framework to create Algospeak-style variations with varying modulation levels and applied it to a COVID-19 disinformation dataset. They conducted evaluations with seven language models to test meaning recovery and disinformation detection.
AI.result: Using a dataset of 700 modulated items, the study finds characteristic trade-offs between understandability and modulation. Specific thresholds ("Majority Understandable Modulation") were identified via curve fitting across different strategies and models.
AI.conclusion: The study formalizes the dynamics of linguistic evasion (Algospeak) and provides tools, including a reference dataset and framework, to analyze this underexplored area in content moderation.

title: Neuromorphic visual attention for Sign-language recognition on SpiNNaker
summary: Sign-language recognition has achieved substantial gains in classification accuracy in recent years; however, the latency and power requirements of most existing methods limit their suitability for real-time deployment. Neuromorphic sensing and processing offer an alternative paradigm based on sparse, event-driven computation that supports low-latency and energy-efficient perception. In this work, we introduce an end-to-end neuromorphic architecture for American Sign Language (ASL) fingerspelling recognition that integrates a spiking visual attention mechanism for online region-of-interest extraction with a compact spiking neural network deployed on the SpiNNaker neuromorphic platform. We benchmark the proposed system against two datasets: a synthetically generated event-based version of the Sign Language MNIST dataset and a natively recorded ASL-DVS dataset, whilst providing a comprehensive overview of Sign-language recognition and related work. This work yields competitive performance in simulation (92.27%) and comparable performance on neuromorphic hardware deployment (83.1%), while achieving the most energy-efficient architecture (0.565 mW) and low latency (3 ms) across all benchmarked approaches. Despite its compact design, the system demonstrates the suitability of task-dependent visual attention applications for edge deployment.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a neuromorphic system for real-time and energy-efficient American Sign Language (ASL) fingerspelling recognition.
AI.motivation: Current sign-language recognition methods are limited by high latency and power consumption, making them less suitable for real-time deployment.
AI.method: Developed an end-to-end neuromorphic architecture using spiking visual attention and a compact spiking neural network on the SpiNNaker platform.
AI.result: Achieved 92.27% accuracy in simulation, 83.1% accuracy on hardware, with 0.565 mW energy consumption, and 3 ms latency.
AI.conclusion: The study highlights the use of neuromorphic sensing for low-latency, energy-efficient ASL recognition, demonstrating potential for edge deployment.

title: SANEmerg: An Emergent Communication Framework for Semantic-aware Agentic AI Networking
comment: Accepted at IEEE/IFIP WiOpt Workshop, Columbus, OH, USA, June 2026
summary: Future networking systems are envisioned to become part of an agentic AI-native ecosystem in which a vast number of heterogeneous and specialized AI agents cooperate seamlessly to fulfill complex user requirements in real time. However, traditional networking paradigms are characterized by a rigid decoupling of communication and computation, which often leads to significant inefficiencies in large-scale agentic AI networking (AgentNet) systems. Emergent communication offers a novel solution by enabling autonomous agents that support task-specific signaling protocols for information exchange and collaborative coordination. In this paper, we consider a multi-agent emergent communication framework, tailored for semantic-aware AgentNet systems in which the user's semantic intent can be automatically detected, inferred, and linked to a set of sub-tasks to be assigned to a set of agents. We investigate how communication and signaling protocols can emerge among collaborative agents with computationally bounded intelligence under stringent bandwidth constraints. Our proposed framework, called SANEmerg, is designed to facilitate the emergence of communication for collaborative task fulfillment while adhering to the physical limits of AgentNet. SANEmerg incorporates a bandwidth-adaptable importance-filter that dynamically prioritizes the transmission of higher-contribution message dimensions, ensuring robust performance in bandwidth-limited environments. Furthermore, SANEmerg integrates a complexity-regularizer grounded in the Minimum Description Length (MDL) principle to facilitate the emergence of computationally bounded signaling. Evaluated via an AgentNet prototype and extensive experimentation, SANEmerg demonstrates significant performance improvements over state-of-the-art solutions, achieving superior task accuracy while significantly reducing bandwidth and computational overhead.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SANEmerg, a multi-agent emergent communication framework for semantic-aware networking systems, addressing inefficiencies in communication and computation for AI networking agents.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address inefficiencies in traditional networking paradigms that lack integration of communication and computation, which hinders performance in AI-native ecosystems with complex agent collaboration.
AI.method: The authors propose SANEmerg, which includes a bandwidth-adaptable importance-filter for efficient message transmission and uses a complexity-regularizer based on the MDL principle to manage signaling complexity.
AI.result: SANEmerg was tested on an AgentNet prototype, showing significantly improved task accuracy, reduced bandwidth usage, and lower computational overhead compared to existing approaches.
AI.conclusion: The study presents SANEmerg as a robust solution for emergent communication in agentic AI-native ecosystems, optimizing task accuracy and resource efficiency under constrained conditions.

title: Enabling Federated Inference via Unsupervised Consensus Embedding
comment: 18 pages, 15 figures, submitted to IEEE Transactions on Mobile Computing (TMC) (under review)
summary: Cooperative inference across independently deployed machine learning models is increasingly desirable in distributed environments, as there is a growing need to leverage multiple models while keeping their data and model parameters private. However, existing cooperative frameworks typically rely on sharing input data, model parameters, or a common encoder, which limits their applicability in privacy-sensitive or cross-organizational settings. To address this challenge, we propose Consensus Embedding-based Federated Inference (CE-FI), a framework that enables pretrained models to cooperate at inference time without sharing model parameters or raw inputs and without assuming a common encoder. CE-FI introduces two components: a Consensus Embedding (CE) layer that maps heterogeneous intermediate representations into a common embedding space, and a Cooperative Output (CO) layer that produces predictions from these embeddings. Both layers are trained using shared unlabeled data only, so the cooperative stage does not require additional labeled data. Experiments on image classification benchmarks -- CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 -- under diverse non-IID conditions show that CE-FI consistently outperforms solo inference and performs comparably to conventional methods that require stronger sharing assumptions. Additional evaluations on text and time-series tasks indicate applicability beyond image classification, although performance depends on the ensemble strategy. Further analysis identifies representation alignment as the primary bottleneck.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes CE-FI, a method for cooperative inference across independent ML models without sharing inputs, model parameters, or encoders.
AI.motivation: Address challenges in cooperative inference for privacy-sensitive and cross-organizational settings by removing the dependency on shared inputs or common encoders.
AI.method: Introduces CE-FI, which uses a Consensus Embedding (CE) layer for mapping intermediate representations into a common space, and a Cooperative Output (CO) layer for predictions, trained using only shared unlabeled data.
AI.result: CE-FI performs better than solo inference and is competitive with methods requiring stronger sharing assumptions in tasks like image classification (CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100). Also applicable to text and time-series tasks, though dependent on ensemble strategy.
AI.conclusion: CE-FI enables privacy-preserving cooperative inference between pretrained models and has broad applicability, though representation alignment remains a challenge.

title: Parser agreement and disagreement in L2 Korean UD: Implications for human-in-the-loop annotation
comment: To be published in the 20th Linguistic Annotation Workshop
summary: We propose a simplified human-in-the-loop workflow for second language (L2) Korean morphosyntactic annotation by leveraging agreement between two domain-adapted parsers. We first evaluate whether parser agreement can serve as a proxy for annotation correctness by comparing it with independent human judgments. The results show strong correspondence between parser and human judgments, supporting the feasibility of semi-automatic L2-Korean UD annotation. Further analysis demonstrates that parser disagreements cluster in linguistically predictable domains such as grammatical-relation distinctions and clause-boundary ambiguity. While many disagreement cases are tractable for iterative model refinement, others reflect deeper representational challenges inherent in parsing and tagging L2-Korean corpora.

AI.tldr: The study introduces a semi-automated process for annotating second-language Korean morphosyntactic annotations using two domain-adapted parsers to simplify workflows, showing promising results in correspondence with human judgments.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to streamline and reduce the manual effort required in annotating second-language (L2) Korean morphosyntactic structures by exploring a semi-automated approach.
AI.method: The authors leveraged agreement between two domain-adapted parsers to evaluate its effectiveness as a proxy for human annotation correctness, comparing parser agreement against human judgments.
AI.result: Strong alignment was found between parser agreement and human annotations, validating the semi-automatic UD annotation approach. Disagreement cases revealed linguistically salient areas for refinement, such as grammatical-relation distinctions and clause-boundary ambiguities.
AI.conclusion: The findings support the feasibility of semi-automatic annotation for L2-Korean morphosyntactic structures, while highlighting areas where linguistic complexity challenges deeper model refinement.

title: Adding Thermal Awareness to Visual Systems in Real-Time via Distilled Diffusion Models
summary: Purely RGB-based vision models often fail to provide reliable cues in challenging scenarios such as nighttime and fog, leading to degraded performance and safety risks. Infrared imaging captures heat-emitting sources and provides critical complementary information, but existing high-fidelity fusion methods suffer from prohibitive latency, rendering them impractical for real-time edge deployment. To address this, we propose FusionProxy, a real-time image fusion module designed as a fully independent, plug-and-play component with diffusion level quality. FusionProxy exploits two complementary statistics of a teacher sample ensemble: per-pixel variance in raw image space, used to weight pixel-level supervision, and per-pixel variance inside frozen foundation backbones, used to route feature-level alignment spatially. Once trained, FusionProxy can be directly integrated into any visual perception system without joint optimization. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method achieves superior performance on static recognition tasks and significantly enhances robustness in dynamic tasks, including closed-loop autonomous driving. Crucially, FusionProxy achieves real-time inference speeds on diverse platforms, from high-end GPUs to commodity hardware, providing a flexible and generalizable solution for all-day perception.

AI.tldr: FusionProxy is a real-time image fusion module designed for integrating RGB and infrared imaging, offering high-quality fusion with low latency for edge deployment.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of RGB-based vision models in challenging conditions like nighttime and fog, while mitigating the high latency of existing fusion methods.
AI.method: FusionProxy uses per-pixel variance from raw image space for weighted supervision and variance from frozen backbone features for spatial alignment. It is a plug-and-play module compatible with any visual perception system.
AI.result: The method achieved superior performance in static recognition tasks and improved robustness in dynamic tasks such as autonomous driving, with real-time inference on diverse hardware.
AI.conclusion: FusionProxy provides an effective, flexible, and scalable solution for integrating RGB and infrared imaging into visual perception systems for reliable and real-time operation.

title: XDecomposer: Learning Prior-Free Set Decomposition for Multiphase X-ray Diffraction
summary: Multiphase powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) analysis remains a fundamental bottleneck in structure identification, as real-world synthesis often produces complex mixtures whose constituent phases (components) cannot be reliably disentangled. While recent advances in representation-based crystal retrieval and generation suggest the possibility of inferring structures directly from PXRD, existing approaches largely assume single-phase inputs and break down in multiphase settings. Here, we present XDecomposer, a prior-free framework for joint decomposition and identification of multiphase XRD patterns without requiring candidate phase lists, structural templates, or prior knowledge of phase number. We formulate multiphase diffraction analysis as a set prediction problem, where the model infers an unordered set of phase-resolved components, their mixture proportions, and corresponding structural representations within a unified architecture. A phase-query-driven decomposition mechanism, together with diffraction-consistent physical reconstruction, enables accurate source separation while preserving crystallographic fidelity. Extensive experiments on both simulated and experimental datasets show that XDecomposer substantially improves reconstruction accuracy and phase identification across diverse chemical systems, while maintaining strong generalization to unseen mixtures. These results provide a practical route toward data-driven, source-resolved multiphase XRD analysis and reduce long-standing dependence on prior-guided iteratively phase matching. The code is openly available at https://github.com/Licht0812/XDecomposer

AI.tldr: The paper introduces XDecomposer, a novel framework that enables accurate phase identification and decomposition in multiphase XRD analysis without the need for prior structural information or candidate lists.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the bottleneck in multiphase PXRD analysis, where current methods struggle with complex mixtures and rely heavily on prior knowledge, single-phase assumptions, or iterative matching.
AI.method: XDecomposer formulates multiphase XRD analysis as a set prediction problem. It employs a phase-query-driven decomposition mechanism integrated with diffraction-consistent reconstruction within a unified architecture to infer structural representations and mixture proportions of phases.
AI.result: Experiments on simulated and experimental datasets demonstrate that XDecomposer improves reconstruction accuracy and phase identification substantially, showcasing robust generalization to unseen mixtures.
AI.conclusion: XDecomposer offers a practical, prior-free solution for multiphase PXRD analysis, reducing the dependence on traditional iterative methods and enabling data-driven phase identification with strong generalization capabilities.

title: WARP: A Benchmark for Primal-Dual Warm-Starting of Interior-Point Solvers
summary: Solving AC Optimal Power Flow (AC-OPF) is of central importance in electricity market operations, where interior-point methods (IPMs) such as IPOPT are the standard solvers. A growing body of work uses machine learning to predict primal warm-start iterates, reporting iteration reductions of 30-46\%. We show that these reported gains rest on an inappropriate evaluation baseline: prior methods benchmark against the flat start $V_m = 1, V_a = 0$, whereas the solver's actual default - the variable-bound midpoint $(l+u)/2$ - is near-optimal for log-barrier centrality. Against this corrected baseline, no primal-only warm-start method reduces solver iterations. We trace the failure to a geometric property of interior-point methods: primal prediction accuracy is anticorrelated with convergence speed, and providing the ground-truth optimal solution $x^*$ without dual variables causes the solver to diverge. Oracle experiments establish that the complete primal-dual-barrier state $(x^*, λ^*, z^*, μ^*)$ reduces IPOPT iterations from 23 to 3 - an 85\% reduction that is structurally inaccessible to primal-only methods. To enable rigorous evaluation of warm-start methods on this task, we release a benchmark suite comprising dual-labeled AC-OPF datasets with IPOPT-extracted solutions, a corrected evaluation protocol, and WARP - a topology-conditioned encode-process-decode interaction network that predicts the full interior-point state $(\hat{x}, \hatλ, \hat{z}, \hatμ)$ on the heterogeneous constraint graph. WARP achieves a 76\% reduction in IPOPT iterations while natively accommodating N-1 contingency topology variations without retraining.

AI.tldr: The paper critiques previous warm-start techniques for AC-OPF problems, introduces a mathematically consistent evaluation method, and presents a new method (WARP) that predicts the full interior-point state to significantly improve interior-point solver efficiency.
AI.motivation: To improve the efficiency of solving AC Optimal Power Flow (AC-OPF) problems, as current machine learning-based warm-start methods rely on flawed evaluation baselines and fail to significantly reduce solver iterations in realistic settings.
AI.method: The authors establish a corrected benchmark for warm-start methods by contrasting baseline approaches, conduct oracle experiments on primal-dual-barrier state relevance, and propose WARP, a specialized neural network architecture designed to predict the full interior-point state.
AI.result: They demonstrate that when using WARP, IPOPT iterations are reduced by 76%, outperforming prior methods and showing its robustness to topology variations without retraining.
AI.conclusion: Primal-only warm-start approaches fail under rigorous evaluation baselines. Predicting full primal-dual-barrier states, as implemented by WARP, notably improves solver performance and sets a new standard for evaluation in AC-OPF problems.

title: Cited but Not Verified: Parsing and Evaluating Source Attribution in LLM Deep Research Agents
summary: Large language models (LLMs) power deep research agents that synthesize information from hundreds of web sources into cited reports, yet these citations cannot be reliably verified. Current approaches either trust models to self-cite accurately, risking bias, or employ retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) that does not validate source accessibility, relevance, or factual consistency. We introduce the first source attribution evaluation framework that uses a reproducible AST parser to extract and evaluate inline citations from LLM-generated Markdown reports at scale. Unlike methods that verify claims in isolation, our framework closes the loop by retrieving the actual cited content, enabling human or model evaluators to judge each citation against its source. Citations are evaluated along three dimensions. (1) Link Works verifies URL accessibility, (2) Relevant Content measures topical alignment, and (3) Fact Check validates factual accuracy against source content. We benchmark 14 closed-source and open-source LLMs across three evaluation dimensions using rubric-based LLM-as-a-judge evaluators calibrated through human review. Our results reveal that even the strongest frontier models maintain link validity above 94% and relevance above 80%, yet achieve only 39-77% factual accuracy, while fewer than half of open-source models successfully generate cited reports in a one-shot setting. Ablation studies on research depth show that Fact Check accuracy drops by approximately 42% on average across two frontier models as tool calls scale from 2 to 150, demonstrating that more retrieval does not produce more accurate citations. These findings reveal a critical disconnect between surface-level citation quality and factual reliability, and our framework provides the evaluation infrastructure to assess the disconnect.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a framework for evaluating the reliability of citations in large language model (LLM)-generated reports. It benchmarks LLMs on citation accuracy, relevance, and factual alignment, revealing a discrepancy between valid citations and true factual reliability.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of verifying the reliability, relevance, and factual accuracy of citations in LLM-powered reports, as current approaches fail to sufficiently validate these aspects, resulting in unreliable outputs.
AI.method: An evaluation framework using an AST parser is introduced to extract and assess inline citations in LLM-generated reports along three dimensions: (1) Link Works (URL accessibility), (2) Relevant Content (topical alignment), and (3) Fact Check (factual accuracy). Benchmarking involved LLM-as-a-judge evaluators calibrated by human review.
AI.result: The evaluation of 14 LLMs shows high citation validity (above 94% for links and above 80% for relevance), but factual accuracy remains low (39-77%). Fact-checking accuracy drops significantly with increased retrieval scale, highlighting limitations in citation reliability despite higher citation volume.
AI.conclusion: The framework reveals a critical gap between citation validity and factual reliability in LLM-generated content. The study highlights the need for better citation accuracy in LLM outputs and provides infrastructure to evaluate and address the issue.

title: T2I-VeRW: Part-level Fine-grained Perception for Text-to-Image Vehicle Retrieval
summary: Vehicle Re-identification (Re-ID) aims to retrieve the most similar image to a given query from images captured by non-overlapping cameras. Extending vehicle Re-ID from image-only queries to text-based queries enables retrieval in real-world scenarios where only a witness description of the target vehicle is available. In this paper, we propose PFCVR, a Part-level Fine-grained Cross-modal Vehicle Retrieval model for text-to-image vehicle re-identification. PFCVR constructs locally paired images and texts at the part level and introduces learnable part-query tokens that aggregate both part-specific and full-sentence context before aligning with visual part features. On top of this explicit local alignment, a bi-directional mask recovery module lets each modality reconstruct its masked content under the guidance of the other, implicitly bridging local correspondences into global feature alignment. Furthermore, we construct a new large-scale dataset called T2I-VeRW, which contains 14,668 images covering 1,796 vehicle identities with fine-grained part-level annotations. Experimental results on the T2I-VeRI dataset show that PFCVR achieves 29.2\% Rank-1 accuracy, improving over the best competing method by +3.7\% percentage points. On the newly proposed T2I-VeRW benchmark, PFCVR achieves 55.2\% Rank-1 accuracy, outperforming a comprehensive set of recent state-of-the-art methods. Source code will be released on https://github.com/Event-AHU/Neuromorphic_ReID

AI.tldr: The paper introduces PFCVR, a model for text-to-image vehicle re-identification, combining part-based local and global feature alignment, achieving notable accuracy improvements on existing benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge in vehicle re-identification where only text-based descriptions of a vehicle are available, making retrieval viable in real-world scenarios.
AI.method: The model (PFCVR) aligns text and image features at both local (part-level with learnable tokens) and global levels, supported by a bi-directional mask recovery module for improved feature correspondence. It also introduces a new dataset, T2I-VeRW, with detailed annotations.
AI.result: On the T2I-VeRI dataset, PFCVR achieved 29.2% Rank-1 accuracy (+3.7% improvement over state-of-the-art), and on the newly introduced T2I-VeRW dataset, it achieved 55.2% Rank-1 accuracy, surpassing recent methods.
AI.conclusion: PFCVR effectively advances text-to-image vehicle re-identification by leveraging fine-grained part-level alignments and a novel dataset, establishing a new benchmark for future studies.

title: Agentic, Context-Aware Risk Intelligence in the Internet of Value
summary: The Internet of Value (IoV) is a heterogeneous, partially-trusted network in which the dominant marginal risk is composite (route, sentiment, liquidity, and the policy a system is willing to commit to) rather than a property of any single chain. We argue that a risk primitive adequate for this regime is a composition of five engines: a prediction engine over price, liquidity, volatility, and route health; a Bittensor verification subnet that decentralises and economically scores prediction outputs; a sentiment-fusion engine over text, on-chain flow, and grey-literature feeds; an agentic engine under constitutional, role-bound action constraints; and an API-risk and scenario engine that converts forecasts into pre-committed action programs in the sense of Monte-Carlo scenario generation. We anchor the architecture in two empirical artefacts: a 27-hour policy-constrained liquidity stress-response experiment on Solana, and a 168-hour prediction-router calibration arc reported with explicit class-imbalance honesty. The case study supports deployability; the validator-loss decomposition is stated formally and is falsifiable.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a risk primitive for the Internet of Value networks, composed of five engines integrating predictions, decentralised verification, sentiment analysis, agentic constraints, and API-risk management. Empirical results from experiments support its deployability.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address the composite marginal risks in heterogeneous Internet of Value networks where risks arise from route, sentiment, liquidity, and system policies, rather than individual chain properties.
AI.method: Five interconnected engines are designed: prediction engine, Bittensor verification subnet, sentiment-fusion engine, agentic engine, and API-risk scenario engine. These are tested via liquidity stress-response and prediction-router calibration experiments.
AI.result: Empirical artifacts include a stress-response experiment on Solana and prediction-router calibration with explicit honesty about imbalances, showing the feasibility of deployability.
AI.conclusion: The proposed risk primitive framework is capable of managing composite risks in IoV networks and is validated through structured experiments, making it a deployable approach for real-world applications.

title: CRAFT: Forgetting-Aware Intervention-Based Adaptation for Continual Learning
summary: Large language models (LLMs) can acquire new capabilities through fine-tuning, but continual adaptation often leads to catastrophic forgetting. We propose CRAFT, a continual learning framework that avoids updating model weights by instead learning low-rank interventions on hidden representations. CRAFT proceeds in three stages: it first routes each task to a group of similar tasks based on output-distribution divergence; it then fine-tunes the model using a Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence against the group's prior state, which directly controls forgetting and determines convergence; finally, it merges interventions for the updated task into the shared representation using the same KL signal. This design unifies routing, regularization, and merging through a single KL-based objective. CRAFT improves overall performance and reduces forgetting compared to strong LoRA-based approaches across multiple benchmarks and model scales, while remaining robust to task ordering. These results suggest that controlling adaptation in representation space, guided by output-space divergence, provides a scalable and principled approach to continual learning in LLMs.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CRAFT, a novel continual learning framework for LLMs that reduces catastrophic forgetting by learning low-rank interventions instead of updating model weights.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of catastrophic forgetting in large language models during continual learning and adaptation.
AI.method: CRAFT uses a combination of routing tasks to shared groups, KL-divergence-based fine-tuning, and merging interventions in the representation space guided by KL objectives.
AI.result: CRAFT outperforms strong LoRA-based methods, improving performance, reducing forgetting across benchmarks, and showing robustness to task ordering.
AI.conclusion: Routing tasks and controlling adaptation in the representation space with KL-divergence offers a scalable, effective way to enable continual learning for LLMs.

title: StraTA: Incentivizing Agentic Reinforcement Learning with Strategic Trajectory Abstraction
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used as interactive agents, but optimizing them for long-horizon decision making remains difficult because current methods are largely purely reactive, which weakens both exploration and credit assignment over extended trajectories. In this work, we present Strategic Trajectory Abstraction (StraTA), a simple framework that introduces an explicit trajectory-level strategy into agentic reinforcement learning (RL). StraTA samples a compact strategy from the initial task state, conditions subsequent actions on that strategy, and trains strategy generation and action execution jointly with a hierarchical GRPO-style rollout design, further enhanced by diverse strategy rollout and critical self-judgment. Experiments on ALFWorld, WebShop, and SciWorld show that StraTA consistently improves both sample efficiency and final performance over strong baselines. StraTA reaches success rates of 93.1% on ALFWorld and 84.2% on WebShop. On SciWorld, StraTA attains a 63.5% overall score, outperforming frontier closed-source models.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Strategic Trajectory Abstraction (StraTA), a framework for enhancing long-horizon decision-making in large language models (LLMs) used as interactive agents.
AI.motivation: Optimizing LLMs for tasks requiring long-horizon decision-making is difficult due to their reactive nature, which limits exploration and effective credit assignment over extended sequences.
AI.method: StraTA introduces trajectory-level strategies into reinforcement learning (RL). It uses hierarchical GRPO-style rollouts, diverse strategy rollout, and self-critical judgment to jointly train strategy generation and action execution.
AI.result: In experiments, StraTA significantly improves sample efficiency and performance, achieving success rates of 93.1% on ALFWorld, 84.2% on WebShop, and outperforming closed-source models with 63.5% on SciWorld.
AI.conclusion: StraTA demonstrates superior long-horizon capabilities and boosts performance in complex interactive RL environments, improving upon existing baselines.

title: PlotPick: AI-powered batch extraction of numerical data from scientific figures
comment: 7 pages, 2 figures, 2 tables. Software available at https://plotpick.streamlit.app and https://github.com/tommycarstensen/plotpick
summary: Systematic reviews and meta-analyses frequently require numerical data that authors report only as figures, yet manual digitisation is slow and does not scale. We present PlotPick, an open-source tool that uses vision-language models (VLMs) to batch-extract structured tabular data from scientific figures. We evaluate six VLMs from three providers on two established chart-to-table benchmarks (ChartX and PlotQA) and compare against the dedicated chart-to-table model DePlot. All six VLMs outperform DePlot on both benchmarks. On ChartX (restricted to bar charts, line charts, box plots, and histograms; n=300), VLMs achieve 88-96% recall versus 71% for DePlot. On PlotQA (n=529), VLMs achieve 86-99% RMSF1 versus 94% for DePlot. The gap is largest on chart types absent from the dedicated models' training data: on box plots, DePlot achieves 24% RMSF1 while VLMs achieve 83-97%. PlotPick is available at https://plotpick.streamlit.app.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces PlotPick, an open-source tool leveraging vision-language models (VLMs) to efficiently extract tabular data from scientific charts. VLMs significantly outperform a specialized model, DePlot, across benchmarks.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to address the inefficiency and scalability issues of manual data digitization from scientific figures, which are essential for systematic reviews and meta-analyses.
AI.method: They evaluate six vision-language models against the dedicated chart-to-table model DePlot using two benchmarks, ChartX and PlotQA, which include diverse chart types. Performance metrics such as recall and RMSF1 were used for comparison.
AI.result: VLMs consistently outperform DePlot across all tested benchmarks and chart types. Notably, VLMs perform exceptionally well on chart types not included in DePlot's training data, showcasing higher generalization capabilities.
AI.conclusion: PlotPick harnesses the power of VLMs to streamline structured data extraction from scientific figures, outperforming dedicated models and demonstrating scalability and flexibility across chart types.

title: Null Space Constrained Contrastive Visual Forgetting for MLLM Unlearning
summary: The core challenge of machine unlearning is to strike a balance between target knowledge removal and non-target knowledge retention. In the context of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), this challenge becomes even more pronounced, as knowledge is further divided into visual and textual modalities that are tightly intertwined. In this paper, we introduce an MLLM unlearning approach that aims to forget target visual knowledge while preserving non-target visual knowledge and all textual knowledge. Specifically, we freeze the LLM backbone and achieve unlearning by fine-tuning the visual module. First, we propose a Contrastive Visual Forgetting (CVF) mechanism to separate target visual knowledge from retained visual knowledge, guiding the representations of target visual concepts toward appropriate regions in the feature space. Second, we identify the null space associated with retained knowledge and constrain the unlearning process within this space, thereby significantly mitigating degradation in knowledge retention. Third, beyond static unlearning scenarios, we extend our approach to continual unlearning, where forgetting requests arrive sequentially. Extensive experiments across diverse benchmarks demonstrate that our approach achieves a strong balance between effective forgetting and robust knowledge retention.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a method for machine unlearning in multimodal large language models (MLLMs) by selectively forgetting visual knowledge while retaining non-target knowledge.
AI.motivation: The challenge is to selectively forget specific visual knowledge in MLLMs while preserving non-target visual knowledge and textual knowledge, due to the intertwined nature of multimodal representations.
AI.method: The proposed method freezes the LLM backbone and fine-tunes the visual module with three components: (1) a Contrastive Visual Forgetting mechanism for separating target from retained visual knowledge in feature space, (2) constraints within the null space of retained knowledge to prevent degradation, and (3) an extension to continual unlearning for handling sequential forgetting requests.
AI.result: The experiments demonstrate the method's effectiveness in achieving a balance between forgetting target knowledge and retaining non-target knowledge across diverse benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach offers a robust solution for machine unlearning in MLLMs, ensuring effective forgetting with minimal impact on retained knowledge.

title: CoMemNet: Contrastive Sampling with Memory Replay Network for Continual Traffic Prediction
summary: In recent years, the integration of non-topological space modeling with temporal learning methods has emerged as an effective approach for capturing spatio-temporal information in non-Euclidean graphs. However, most existing methods rely on static underlying graph structures, which are inadequate for capturing the continuously expanding and evolving patterns in streaming traffic networks. To address this challenge, we propose a simple yet efficient dual-branch continual learning framework for traffic prediction, named CoMemNet. The fast-converging Online branch undertakes the primary prediction tasks, while the momentum-updated Target branch extracts historical information using Wasserstein Distance features to create a Dynamic Contrastive Sampler (DC Sampler). This sampler selects a node set with significant dynamic network feature changes for training, effectively mitigating the issue of catastrophic forgetting. Additionally, the backbone incorporates a lightweight Node-Adaptive Temporal Memory Buffer (TMRB-N) to consolidate old knowledge through memory replay and address the risk of memory explosion. Finally, we provide two newly curated open-source datasets. Experimental results demonstrate that CoMemNet achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance across all three large-scale real-world datasets. The code is available at: https://github.com/meiwu5/CoMemNet.

AI.tldr: This paper presents CoMemNet, a dual-branch continual learning framework for traffic prediction in dynamic networks, addressing catastrophic forgetting and memory management issues while achieving state-of-the-art performance.
AI.motivation: Existing spatio-temporal learning methods fall short in handling continuously evolving patterns in streaming traffic networks due to their reliance on static graph structures.
AI.method: CoMemNet comprises an Online branch for fast prediction tasks and a Target branch using Wasserstein Distance for dynamic contrastive sampling. It also includes a lightweight Node-Adaptive Temporal Memory Buffer (TMRB-N) for memory replay.
AI.result: CoMemNet achieves SOTA results on three large-scale real-world datasets and is supported by two newly curated datasets.
AI.conclusion: CoMemNet effectively addresses the challenges in streaming traffic networks, including catastrophic forgetting and memory explosion, and offers a novel approach for continuous spatio-temporal learning.

title: Beyond Negative Rollouts: Positive-Only Policy Optimization with Implicit Negative Gradients
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR), due to the deterministic verification, becomes a dominant paradigm for enhancing the reasoning ability of large language models (LLMs). The community witnesses the rapid change from the Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) to Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), in which GRPO reduces the complicated advantage estimation with simple estimation over grouped positive and negative rollouts. However, we note that negative rollouts may admit no gradation of failure severity, and the combinatorial vastness makes penalizing a few sampled negatives unlikely to cover a meaningful reward signal under sparse binary rewards. In this work, we propose Positive-Only Policy Optimization (POPO), a novel RLVR framework in which learning can occur exclusively via online positive rollouts. Specifically, POPO utilizes bounded importance sampling over the positive rollout set. Thus, no disjoint negative rollouts are used for the gradient guidance. We show that implicit negative gradients can emerge naturally through reinforcing the positive probability via rollouts redistribution. Next, POPO stabilizes the policy optimization through two mechanisms. First, it applies a siamese policy network with a momentum-based adaptation law for stabilized policy evolution. Second, we replace the KL-divergence with a bounded similarity penalty term in the siamese representation space. We conduct extensive experiments using publicly available, well-established text-LLM models, e.g., the Qwen family, across all-level mathematical benchmarks. Our experiment demonstrates that POPO achieves performance comparable to, or even superior to GRPO. Notably, we show that POPO can achieve 36.67% in AIME 2025 with Qwen-Math-7B, outperforming GRPO 30.00%. Our ablation and sweep studies further illustrate the necessity and robustness of POPO components.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Positive-Only Policy Optimization (POPO), a new framework for reinforcement learning with large language models, achieving notable performance improvements without utilizing negative rollouts.
AI.motivation: To address limitations in GRPO, particularly the ineffective penalization of negative rollouts and challenges in sparse binary rewards.
AI.method: POPO relies exclusively on positive rollouts, using bounded importance sampling and stabilizing mechanisms like a siamese policy network with momentum-based adaptation and a bounded similarity penalty.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate that POPO achieves better performance compared to GRPO, including a significant improvement in AIME 2025 with Qwen-Math-7B.
AI.conclusion: POPO offers a robust alternative to GRPO for RLVR, showcasing stability and superior performance by exclusively focusing on positive rollouts.

title: Domain Generalization through Spatial Relation Induction over Visual Primitives
summary:
Domain generalization requires identifying stable representations that support reliable classification across domains. Most existing methods seek such stability through improving the training process, for example, through model selection strategies, data augmentation, or feature-alignment objectives. Although these strategies can be effective, they leave the representation learning of structural composition implicit, which may limit performance on compositional domain generalization benchmarks.
  In this work, we propose Primitive-Aware Relational Structure for domain gEneralization (PARSE), an image classification framework that factors visual recognition into visual primitives and their relational composition. We represent these compositions using soft binary, ternary, and quaternary predicates over primitive locations, yielding differentiable measures of spatial alignment that can be learned end-to-end. To learn primitives and relational structures jointly, we design an end-to-end architecture with three components: (1) a convolutional neural network (CNN) backbone that extracts general visual features, (2) a concept bottleneck layer that maps these features to primitive heatmaps with differentiable spatial coordinates, and (3) a structural scoring layer that evaluates candidate spatial relations among the detected primitives. We then compute class probability from the joint evidence of its class-specific relational compositions.
  Across CUB-DG and the DomainBed benchmark suite,PARSE improves accuracy by over 4.5 percentage points on CUB-DG and remains competitive with existing DG methods on DomainBed.

AI.tldr: PARSE is a novel framework designed to improve domain generalization through visual primitive extraction and relational composition, achieving significant improvements in classification accuracy.
AI.motivation: Existing domain generalization methods implicitly learn structural representations of visual elements, which can limit their ability to address compositional domain generalization challenges.
AI.method: PARSE employs a three-component architecture: a CNN backbone for feature extraction, a concept bottleneck layer for primitive heatmap generation, and a structural scoring layer to evaluate spatial relations among primitives, all learned end-to-end.
AI.result: PARSE achieved over 4.5 percentage point improvements on the CUB-DG benchmark and remained competitive against existing methods in the DomainBed suite.
AI.conclusion: By explicitly modeling visual primitives and their relational composition, PARSE enhances domain generalization performance and offers a promising path for addressing compositional challenges.

title: PREFER: Personalized Review Summarization with Online Preference Learning
summary: Product reviews significantly influence purchasing decisions on e-commerce platforms. However, the sheer volume of reviews can overwhelm users, obscuring the information most relevant to their specific needs. Current e-commerce summarization systems typically produce generic, static summaries that fail to account for the fact that (i) different users care about different product characteristics, and (ii) these preferences may evolve with interactions. To address the challenge of unknown latent preferences, we propose an online learning framework that generates personalized summaries for each user. Our system iteratively refines its understanding of user preferences by incorporating feedback directly from the generated summaries over time. We provide a case study using the Amazon Reviews'23 dataset, showing in controlled simulations that online preference learning improves alignment with target user interests while maintaining summary quality.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces an online learning framework to generate personalized summaries of product reviews based on user preferences, which evolve through iterative feedback.
AI.motivation: To address the issue of overwhelming amounts of product reviews making it difficult for users to find relevant information tailored to their specific needs.
AI.method: An online learning framework is developed that generates personalized summaries by understanding and iteratively refining user preferences through summary-driven feedback.
AI.result: Controlled simulations with the Amazon Reviews'23 dataset demonstrate improved alignment with user interests while retaining high summary quality.
AI.conclusion: The proposed system successfully adapts to users' latent preferences over time, enabling more relevant and personalized review summaries.

title: Multi-Dimensional Behavioral Evaluation of Agentic Stock Prediction Systems Using LLM Judges with Closed-Loop Reinforcement Learning Feedback
comment: 9 pages, 2 figures, 8 tables. Short Communication submitted to Knowledge-Based Systems (Elsevier)
summary: Agentic stock prediction systems make sequences of interdependent decisions (regime detection, pathway routing, reinforcement learning control) whose individual quality is hidden by aggregate metrics such as mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) or directional accuracy. We present a behavioral evaluation framework that addresses this gap. Behavioral traces logged at every autonomous decision point are grouped into five-day episodes and scored along six domain-specific dimensions (regime detection, routing, adaptation, risk calibration, strategy coherence, error recovery) by an ensemble of three large language model (LLM) judges (GPT 5.4, Claude 4.6 Opus, Gemini 3.1 Pro). Perturbation-based validation on 420 episodes yields targeted score drops of $-1.6$ to $-2.4$ on intended dimensions versus an average of $-0.32$ on the remaining five, with cross-model agreement up to Krippendorff's $α= 0.85$. The composite behavioral score, used here only for cross-episode reporting, correlates at $ρ= 0.72$ with realized 20-day Sharpe ratio from offline backtesting. Closing the loop, the framework converts deficient per-dimension scores into a credit-assigned penalty term added to the Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) reward. Three short fine-tuning cycles, all confined to the validation period, produce on the held-out 2017-2025 test period a one-day MAPE reduction from 0.61% to 0.54% (an 11.5% relative reduction; $p<0.001$, Cohen's $d=0.31$), a directional accuracy increase from 71% to 74%, and an 18% Sharpe ratio improvement (95% bootstrap CI [8.2%, 27.4%]), with gains concentrated in high-volatility episodes where the original system was most behaviorally deficient. Results are from offline backtesting and do not address effects specific to live deployment.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a behavioral evaluation framework for stock prediction systems, using LLM judges to score key decision-making dimensions and refining models based on deficiencies, resulting in improvements in prediction accuracy and stability during backtesting.
AI.motivation: Traditional stock prediction systems often rely on aggregate metrics that fail to capture the quality of individual decision-making steps. The paper aims to address this gap by evaluating autonomous decision-making behaviors in detail.
AI.method: Agentic stock prediction systems were evaluated through a framework logging behavioral data at every decision point, grouped into episodes, and scored across six dimensions by LLM judges. Deficient scores were converted into penalty terms to fine-tune the Soft Actor-Critic model.
AI.result: Fine-tuning cycles resulted in reduced one-day MAPE (mean absolute percentage error), improved directional accuracy, and enhanced Sharpe ratios during offline backtesting on the test period. Gains were particularly significant in high-volatility conditions.
AI.conclusion: The behavioral evaluation framework enables targeted improvements in stock prediction models, showing promise for refining decision-making quality, though results remain limited to backtesting settings without considering live deployment factors.

title: EMO: Pretraining Mixture of Experts for Emergent Modularity
summary: Large language models are typically deployed as monolithic systems, requiring the full model even when applications need only a narrow subset of capabilities, e.g., code, math, or domain-specific knowledge. Mixture-of-Experts (MoEs) seemingly offer a potential alternative by activating only a subset of experts per input, but in practice, restricting inference to a subset of experts for a given domain leads to severe performance degradation. This limits their practicality in memory-constrained settings, especially as models grow larger and sparser. We introduce EMO, an MoE designed for modularity-the independent use and composition of expert subsets-without requiring human-defined priors. Our key idea is to encourage tokens from similar domains to rely on similar experts. Since tokens within a document often share a domain, EMO restricts them to select experts from a shared pool, while allowing different documents to use different pools. This simple constraint enables coherent expert groupings to emerge during pretraining using document boundaries alone. We pretrain a 1B-active, 14B-total EMO on 1T tokens. As a full model, it matches standard MoE performance. Crucially, it enables selective expert use: retaining only 25% (12.5%) of experts incurs just a 1% (3%) absolute drop, whereas standard MoEs break under the same setting. We further find that expert subsets in EMO specialize at semantic levels (e.g., domains such as math or code), in contrast to the low-level syntactic specialization observed in standard MoEs. Altogether, our results demonstrate a path toward modular, memory-efficient deployment of large, sparse models and open new opportunities for composable architectures.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces EMO, a new Mixture-of-Experts model that maintains high performance even when utilizing selective subsets of experts, enabling memory-efficient deployment in domain-specific tasks.
AI.motivation: Standard large language models demand considerable computational resources and cannot easily specialize in narrow domains. Existing Mixture-of-Experts models degrade in performance when constrained to subsets of experts, limiting their utility in modular and memory-efficient applications.
AI.method: EMO introduces a mechanism for grouping tokens with similar domains to rely on shared pools of experts during pretraining. It uses document boundaries to enforce modularity without predefined human annotations, achieving emergent expert specialization.
AI.result: EMO matches the performance of standard MoEs when fully utilized, but retains robustness when using selective expert subsets. For example, retaining only 25% of experts results in just a 1% performance drop, whereas standard MoEs fail under similar conditions.
AI.conclusion: EMO is a promising approach for deploying modular and memory-efficient large language models. It enables domain-specific expert specialization and composable architectures with minimal performance trade-offs.

title: Fusion in Your Way: Aligning Image Fusion with Heterogeneous Demands via Direct Preference Optimization
summary: As a key technique in multi-modal processing, infrared and visible image fusion (IVIF) plays a crucial role in integrating complementary spectral information for visual enhancement and downstream vision tasks. Despite remarkable progress, existing methods struggle to flexibly accommodate heterogeneous demands. Achieving adaptive fusion that aligns with various preferences from both human and machine vision remains an open and challenging problem. To address this challenge, we propose DPOFusion, a direct preference optimization (DPO) framework integrating the property-aligned latent diffusion model (PALDM) and the preference-controllable latent diffusion model (PCLDM), enabling task-guided, preference-adaptive IVIF for both human and machine vision. The PALDM leverages a latent fusion prior and a joint conditional loss to generate diverse candidate fusion results with various properties. PCLDM is subsequently fine-tuned via instance direct preference optimization (IDPO), enabling direct control of the final fusion results with heterogeneous preference signals. Experimental results demonstrate that our framework not only attains precise preference alignment among humans, vision-language models, and task-driven networks, but also sets a new benchmark for adaptive fusion quality and task-oriented transferability.

AI.tldr: DPOFusion framework addresses adaptive fusion needs in infrared and visible image fusion by aligning preferences for both human and machine vision using advanced diffusion models.
AI.motivation: Existing IVIF techniques struggle to meet diverse and heterogenous demands for human and machine vision applications.
AI.method: DPOFusion integrates latent diffusion models (PALDM and PCLDM), leveraging direct preference optimization for creating adaptable fusion results.
AI.result: The framework delivers precise preference alignment across human and machine vision scenarios, achieving superior fusion quality and task-transferability.
AI.conclusion: DPOFusion sets a benchmark for adaptive IVIF quality and flexibility, addressing challenges in preference-guided image fusion effectively.

title: Wisteria: A Unified Multi-Scale Feature Learning Framework for DNA Language Model
summary: DNA language model aims to decipher the regulatory grammar and semantic of genomes by capturing long range dependencies in DNA sequences. Existing methods emphasize long range token interactions but often ignore the interplay between local motifs and global dependencies. In this paper, we propose Wisteria, a genomic language model that integrates multi scale feature learning within a unified framework for DNA sequence. Specifically, Wisteria augments the Mamba based architecture with gated dilated convolutions to capture local motifs and regulatory patterns, while gated multilayer perceptrons refine global dependencies. We further introduce a Fourier based attention mechanism to support frequency domain modeling, periodic extension and length generalization. Across four experimental settings with both short and long range dependencies, Wisteria demonstrates strong performance on downstream benchmarks against competitive DNA language model baselines. These results indicate that Wisteria effectively unifies local and global dependency modeling for multi scale genomic sequence analysis.

AI.tldr: Wisteria is a genomic language model integrating multi-scale feature learning to unify local and global dependency modeling in DNA sequences.
AI.motivation: To address the gap in DNA language models that focus on long-range interactions but neglect the interplay between local motifs and global dependencies.
AI.method: The model uses gated dilated convolutions for local motif extraction, gated multilayer perceptrons for global dependencies, and Fourier-based attention for modeling in the frequency domain.
AI.result: Wisteria exhibits strong performance across benchmarks, outperforming other DNA language model baselines in short and long-range dependency tasks.
AI.conclusion: Wisteria effectively bridges local and global dependency modeling, enhancing multi-scale genomic sequence analysis.

title: Weak-to-Strong Generalization is Nearly Inevitable (in Linear Models)
summary: Weak-to-strong generalization is a phenomenon in post-training whereby a strong student model, when finetuned solely with feedback from a weaker teacher, can not only surpass the teacher, but can improve upon its own capabilities. Recent work of Burns et al. (2023) demonstrated that this can occur in the setting of frontier language models, and subsequently there has been a flurry of both empirical work trying to exploit this phenomenon, as well as theoretical work attempting to understand it. In this work, we demonstrate that weak-to-strong generalization occurs in standard linear logistic regression, under mild distributional assumptions on the data. In fact, we show that this happens for most student-teacher pairs, suggesting that weak-to-strong generalization is in fact \emph{almost inevitable}, even in this basic setting. Notably, our setting does not require the student to be more expressive or have more model capacity in any way compared to the teacher, which runs contrary to the prevailing theoretical belief that a mismatch in model capacity is a central mechanism to weak-to-strong generalization.

AI.tldr: This paper finds that weak-to-strong generalization, where a stronger student outperforms its weaker teacher after finetuning, is almost inevitable in linear logistic regression under mild conditions.
AI.motivation: To understand and establish theoretical foundations for the weak-to-strong generalization phenomenon observed in machine learning, where stronger models improve despite using feedback from weaker counterparts.
AI.method: The authors analyze standard linear logistic regression and investigate the generalization phenomenon under mild distributional assumptions, not requiring the student model to surpass the teacher in capacity.
AI.result: Weak-to-strong generalization is shown to be inevitable for most student-teacher pairs in linear logistic regression, challenging earlier beliefs about capacity mismatch driving the phenomenon.
AI.conclusion: Weak-to-strong generalization can occur universally even in simple machine learning setups like linear regression, refuting the necessity of model capacity mismatch for such behavior.

title: PRAISE: Prefix-Based Rollout Reuse in Agentic Search Training
summary: In agentic search, large language models (LLMs) are trained to perform multi-turn retrieval and reasoning for complex tasks such as multi-hop question answering (QA). However, current search-based Reinforcement Learning (RL) methods suffer from two core limitations: expensive long-horizon rollouts are under-utilized during training, and supervision is typically available only at the final answer, resulting in severe reward sparsity. We present Prefix-based Rollout reuse for Agentic search with Intermediate Step rEwards (PRAISE), a framework for improving both data efficiency and credit assignment in agentic search training. Given a complete search trajectory, PRAISE extracts prefix states at different search turns, elicits intermediate answers from them, and uses these prefixes both to construct additional training trajectories and to derive step-level rewards from performance differences across prefixes. Our method uses a single shared model for both search policy learning and prefix answer evaluation, enabling joint optimization without extra human annotations or a separate reward model. Experiments on multi-hop QA benchmarks show that PRAISE consistently improves performance over strong baselines.

AI.tldr: PRAISE enhances training efficiency and reward distribution in agentic search leveraging intermediate steps data.
AI.motivation: Current methods for multi-turn retrieval and reasoning suffer inefficiencies due to underused rollouts and sparse rewards.
AI.method: PRAISE extracts prefix states from search trajectories to optimize training and define fine-grained step-level rewards using performance differentials.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate superior multi-hop QA performance compared to baseline approaches.
AI.conclusion: PRAISE represents a significant enhancement in agentic search by addressing reward sparsity and improving training methodologies.

title: RealCam: Real-Time Novel-View Video Generation with Interactive Camera Control
summary: Camera-controlled video-to-video (V2V) generation enables dynamic viewpoint synthesis from monocular footage, holding immense potential for interactive filmmaking and live broadcasting. However, existing implicit synthesis methods fundamentally rely on non-causal, full-sequence processing and rigid prefix-style temporal concatenation. This architectural paradigm mandates bidirectional attention, resulting in prohibitive computational latency, quadratic complexity scaling, and inherent incompatibility with real-time streaming or variable-length inputs. To overcome these limitations, we introduce \texttt{RealCam}, a novel autoregressive framework for interactive, real-time camera-controlled V2V generation. We first design a high-fidelity teacher model grounded in a \textbf{Cross-frame In-context Learning} paradigm. By interleaving source and target frames into synchronized contextual pairs, our design inherently enables length-agnostic generalization and naturally facilitates causal adaptation, breaking the rigid prefix bottleneck. We then distill this teacher into a few-step causal student via Self-Forcing with Distribution Matching Distillation, enabling efficient, on-the-fly streaming synthesis. Furthermore, to mitigate severe loop inconsistency in closed-loop trajectories, we propose \textbf{Loop-Closed Data Augmentation (LoopAug)}, a novel paradigm that synthesizes globally consistent loop sequences from existing multiview datasets. Extensive experiments demonstrate that \texttt{RealCam} achieves state-of-the-art visual fidelity and temporal consistency while enabling truly interactive camera control with orders-of-magnitude faster inference than existing paradigms. Our project page is at https://xyc-fly.github.io/RealCam/.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes RealCam, an autoregressive framework for real-time, camera-controlled video-to-video (V2V) generation, improving upon existing methods with significant computational efficiency.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of current implicit synthesis methods for camera-controlled V2V generation, such as high latency, quadratic complexity, and incompatibility with real-time and variable-length scenarios.
AI.method: The authors designed a high-fidelity teacher model using a Cross-frame In-context Learning paradigm, combined with a distillation process that generates a causal student model via Self-Forcing with Distribution Matching Distillation. They also introduced Loop-Closed Data Augmentation (LoopAug) to reduce loop inconsistencies.
AI.result: RealCam achieves state-of-the-art visual fidelity and temporal consistency while allowing interactive camera control with significantly faster inference compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: RealCam overcomes major limitations of prior approaches, enabling efficient, real-time V2V synthesis with high visual quality and temporal coherence for applications like interactive filmmaking and live broadcasting.

title: Intentmaking and Sensemaking: Human Interaction with AI-Guided Mathematical Discovery
summary: Artificial intelligence offers powerful new tools for scientific discovery, but the interaction paradigms required to effectively harness these systems remain underexplored. In this paper, we present findings from a formative user study with 11 expert mathematicians who used AlphaEvolve, an evolutionary coding agent, to tackle advanced problems in their fields of expertise. We identify and characterize a distinct workflow we term intentmaking, the iterative process of discovering, defining, and refining one's experimental goals through active system interaction. We frame this as a natural extension to sensemaking, the cognitive process of building an understanding of complex or novel data. We suggest that users enter a cycle of intentmaking (defining and updating their experiment) and sensemaking (interpreting the results) which repeats many times during the course of an investigation. Our documentation of these themes suggests an approach to designing AI tools for scientific discovery that goes beyond the existing question/answer model of many current systems, treating them as collaborative instruments rather than opaque black-box assistants.

AI.tldr: The paper discusses creating AI systems for scientific discovery, focusing on user interaction and introducing a novel workflow called "intentmaking" for iterative goal refinement.
AI.motivation: Current AI systems often treat interaction as a question-answer model, yet scientific discovery demands collaborative tools for deeper engagement.
AI.method: The study involved 11 expert mathematicians using AlphaEvolve, an evolutionary coding agent, to examine its role in facilitating advanced problem-solving.
AI.result: The study identifies the iterative cycle of intentmaking and sensemaking, highlighting how users continually redefine goals and interpret results during investigations.
AI.conclusion: AI tools for scientific discovery should prioritize collaborative workflows, focusing on iterative refinement and understanding rather than simplistic interactions.

title: RVPO: Risk-Sensitive Alignment via Variance Regularization
summary: Current critic-less RLHF methods aggregate multi-objective rewards via an arithmetic mean, leaving them vulnerable to constraint neglect: high-magnitude success in one objective can numerically offset critical failures in others (e.g., safety or formatting), masking low-performing "bottleneck" rewards vital for reliable multi-objective alignment. We propose Reward-Variance Policy Optimization (RVPO), a risk-sensitive framework that penalizes inter-reward variance during advantage aggregation, shifting the objective from "maximize sum" to "maximize consistency." We show via Taylor expansion that a LogSumExp (SoftMin) operator effectively acts as a smooth variance penalty. We evaluate RVPO on rubric-based medical and scientific reasoning with up to 17 concurrent LLM-judged reward signals (Qwen2.5-3B/7B/14B) and on tool-calling with rule-based constraints (Qwen2.5-1.5B/3B). By preventing the model from neglecting difficult constraints to exploit easier objectives, RVPO improves overall scores on HealthBench (0.261 vs. 0.215 for GDPO at 14B, $p < 0.001$) and maintains competitive accuracy on GPQA-Diamond without the late-stage degradation observed in other multi-reward methods, demonstrating that variance regularization mitigates constraint neglect across model scales without sacrificing general capabilities.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces RVPO, a critic-less risk-sensitive RLHF method that addresses reward aggregation issues by penalizing inter-reward variance to enhance consistency and mitigate constraint neglect.
AI.motivation: To tackle constraint neglect issues in critic-less RLHF methods, where high successes in one objective can overshadow critical failures in others, compromising reliable multi-objective alignment.
AI.method: RVPO uses a LogSumExp operator as a smooth variance penalty during reward aggregation, emphasizing consistency in multi-reward policy optimization.
AI.result: RVPO improved performance on HealthBench significantly compared to GDPO, avoided degradation on GPQA-Diamond, and demonstrated effectiveness across model scales.
AI.conclusion: Variance regularization in RVPO prevents constraint neglect, enhances reliability in multi-objective alignment, and maintains competitive accuracy without sacrificing general model capabilities.

title: PersonaGesture: Single-Reference Co-Speech Gesture Personalization for Unseen Speakers
summary: We propose PersonaGesture, a diffusion-based pipeline for single-reference co-speech gesture personalization of unseen speakers. Given target speech and one motion clip from a new speaker, the model must synthesize gestures that follow the new utterance while retaining speaker-specific pose choices, without per-speaker optimization. This setting is useful for avatars and virtual agents, but it is hard because the reference mixes stable speaker habits with utterance-specific trajectories. PersonaGesture consists of two key components, Adaptive Style Infusion (ASI) and Implicit Distribution Rectification (IDR), to separate temporal identity evidence from residual statistic correction. A Style Perceiver first encodes the variable-length reference into compact speaker-memory tokens. ASI injects these tokens into denoising through zero-initialized residual cross-attention, enabling style evidence to affect motion formation without replacing the pretrained speech-to-motion prior. Building on this, IDR applies a length-aware diagonal affine map in latent space to correct residual channel-wise moments estimated from the same reference. Across BEAT2 and ZeroEGGS, we evaluate quantitative metrics, reference-identity controls, same-audio diagnostics, qualitative comparisons, and human preference. Experiments show that separating denoising-time speaker memory from conservative post-generation moment correction improves unseen-speaker personalization over collapsed style codes, full-reference attention, and one-clip finetuning. Project: https://xiangyue-zhang.github.io/PersonaGesture.

AI.tldr: PersonaGesture pipeline personalizes co-speech gestures for unseen speakers using a single reference motion clip, leveraging Adaptive Style Infusion and Implicit Distribution Rectification for improved synthesis.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of generating personalized gestures for unseen virtual avatars or agents using limited reference data without per-speaker optimization.
AI.method: Uses two components: Adaptive Style Infusion (ASR) for injecting speaker-specific styles during denoising and Implicit Distribution Rectification (IDR) for correcting residual motion statistics post-generation.
AI.result: Demonstrates improved personalization for unseen speakers in gesture synthesis across benchmarks like BEAT2 and ZeroEGGS, outperforming alternative methods in various tests.
AI.conclusion: Separating style memory injection and motion correction enhances synthesis of personalized co-speech gestures for unseen speakers using limited reference data.

title: Which Are the Low-Resource Languages of the Semantic Web?
comment: ESWC 2026 - 23rd European Semantic Web Conference, May 2026, Dubrovnik, Croatia
summary: Emerging digital technologies are exacerbating the existing divide in Open Access Data (OAD) between high-and low-resource languages, excluding many communities from the global digital transformation.
